summary:
Shapur I (r. c. 240–270 AD) was the second king of the [[event:2059|Sassanid Empire]] and one of its greatest rulers — a military genius who defeated three Roman emperors in battle, captured one (Valerian, see [[event:2826|Battle of Ctesiphon]]), and pushed the [[event:2825|Roman-Sassanid Wars]] to their most favorable point for Persia, briefly capturing Antioch (the third city of the Roman world) and extending Persian influence deep into Roman territory. Shapur's victories were commemorated in the largest rock-cut relief panels in the ancient world at Naqsh-e Rostam and Bishapur, showing him on horseback with three Roman emperors at his feet or in chains — among the most powerful political images of antiquity.

Shapur's reign established the Sassanid Empire as an equal rival to Rome rather than merely a troublesome eastern neighbor. He organized the administration of his vast empire, patronized learning and religious pluralism (he was relatively tolerant of Christianity, Manichaeism, and other faiths), and reportedly invited Greek physicians and philosophers to Persia after his campaigns — contributing to what would become the Academy of Gondishapur's intellectual tradition. Mani, the prophet of [[event:2080|Manichaeism]], enjoyed royal patronage under Shapur before falling out of favor under his successor. Shapur's inscription at Ka'ba-ye Zartosht, written in three languages (Middle Persian, Parthian, and Greek), is one of the most important historical sources for early Sassanid history and his military campaigns.
